Summary for one primary research article
Due by Lab 12
Explain the question(s), hypothesis and specific prediction of the experiment that the authors were testing
Explain how question was tested (methods/background)
Describe results (include figure or table).  Do these results support the hypothesis?
How do these results relate to the questions asked or link to another study that you will discuss in your paper (conclusion)
Must write in your own words ( no quotes , no plagiarism)
Must cite paper in text and provide complete citation list at end
Must include figure or table (can rework figure or table) at end of summary
Length ~2 pages double-spaced (does not include figures or tables)
Examples in Canvas Lab 11 and 12 Module

Lab 11 Natural Selection and Population Genetics
Evolution of antibiotic resistance in Escherichia coli
Computer simulations of natural selection using PopGen

Evolution of antibiotic resistance in Escherichia coli
Introduction of bacteria and antibiotics
Bacterial resistance to antibiotics
Laboratory evolution of antibiotic resistance

Bacteria
Prokaryotes (simple cell, no nucleus, 1 chromosome)
typically single celled
simple structure
fast-growing (generation time for E. coli : 20 min)
ideal subject for genetic engineering and research
Escherichia coli
Staphylococcus aureus

Two recently divided cells (Binary fission)
Bacteria multiply through binary fission, and have rapid population growth
Two “identical” daughter cells produced, but mutations in DNA may occur and accumulate
Population growth
Cell growth and division

Bacterial growth on agar plates
Agar plates contain nutrients needed for bacterial growth
Sufficient cell isolation will allow a single cell to grow into a discrete group of cells (~10 7 ) called a colony
A colony is considered a pure culture
Confluent growth: Streaks or lawn growth will occur when a high number of cells are inoculated (not pure culture)
Bacterial colonies
(loop)
Bacterial lawn (cotton swab)
Bacterial streaks
(toothpick)

Antibiotics
Antibiotics are naturally produced microbial metabolites (or their derivatives) that kill or inhibit growth of other microorganisms
an example of species competition
[Speaker Notes] Image from: Fleming A (1929) On the antibacterial action of cultures of a Penicillium, with special reference to their use in the isolation of B. influenza. British Journal of Experimental Pathology; 10.

Humans take antibiotics to treat bacterial infections
Used in treating infections (diseases) caused by bacterial pathogens (useless against viral infections)
Antibiotics kill susceptible bacterial cells (pathogens and non-pathogens) in the body
If bacterial cells can survive an antibiotic, then the bacteria are resistant to this antibiotic, which can no longer be used to treat this infection caused by this bacterial pathogen

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
1. Affect envelope of bacterial cells (cell wall, cell membrane)
e.g. ampicillin causes the cell to make a defective cell wall  bacterial cell lyses and dies
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
2. Inhibit microbial metabolism or nucleic acid synthesis
e.g. nalidixic acid blocks DNA gyrase which affects nucleic acid synthesis.  Makes it difficult for bacterial cell to replicate.
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
3. Inhibit protein synthesis
e.g. tetracycline inhibits bacterial ribosomes  bacteria cannot make proteins and die
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

Bacteria can become resistant to antibiotics by acquiring antibiotic resistance genes
Antibiotic resistance genes
1. Code for enzyme that inactivates the drug
2. Modify drug target so that cell is not sensitive to the drug
3. Limit uptake of drug
4. “Efflux pumps” that pump drug out of cell

Each time the antibiotic is used, resistant bacteria multiply and prevail because they are the only ones able to do so under those conditions  the population becomes more resistant
The evolution of antibiotic resistance in a bacterial population

Figure 1
History of Antibiotic Resistance – Resistance evolves shortly after a new antibiotic is introduced
Högberg et al. (2011) Trends in Pharmacological Sciences 31: 509-515
[Speaker Notes] Antibiotic introduction and the development of resistance Adapted from [3,4].
Examples of Increases in Antibiotic Resistance

Impact of Antibiotic Resistance
Available from 2019 CDC Report
https://www.cdc.gov/drugresistance/pdf/threats-report/2019-ar-threats-report-508.pdf [https://www.cdc.gov/drugresistance/pdf/threats-report/2019-ar-threats-report-508.pdf]
[Speaker Notes] 2014 data

Andrei et al (2018) DISCOVERIES 2018, Jan-Mar, 6(1): e81, DOI: 10.15190/d.2018.1
3
2
1
Among FDA approved drugs each year, antibacterial drugs are only a very small fraction

Transfer of antibiotic resistance genes between natural  and clinical environments
Martínez (2008) Science. 321: 365-367.
DOI: 10.1126/science.1159483
[Speaker Notes] Most antibiotic resistance genes acquired through horizontal gene transfer have been originated in environmental microorganisms. In their original host, these determinants (red dot) form part of integrated regulatory and metabolic networks. After their dissemination (circle), their integration in the novel metabolic networks would be difficult, and their only role will be resistance. The reintroduction of plasmid-encoded antibiotic resistance determinants in natural environments, together with the changes they suffer as the consequence of human activities, might be relevant for the future evolution and dissemination of antibiotic resistance determinants in bacterial pathogens.

Escherichia coli : Our organism
Lives in the intestines of animal hosts; used as fecal indicator in water analysis
E. coli in waterways can come from sewage, waterfowl, animal farms, etc.
Prior exposure to antibiotics?

Water sample from St. Joseph River plated on Coliscan plate
Lab 11: check susceptibility to antibiotics
Pure culture obtained on Nutrient agar
Possible E. coli colony confirmed on EMB agar
E. coli were isolated from St. Joseph River

Measuring drug resistance
Note: microbial susceptible/resistant to a drug at varying degrees
Levels of drug resistance can be measured several ways, including the disc diffusion test (Kirby-Bauer test)
For each antibiotic disk, the size of ZOI (diameter) can be used to compare resistance level in different bacteria (smaller ZOI  more resistant)
1. Plate seeded with the organism
2. Antibiotic disks applied
3. Drug diffuses into agar, inhibiting growth
4. After 24 hr incubation, measure zone of inhibition

Which bacterial strain is more drug-resistant?
Strain A
Strain B

Experimental Question Question 1: Do the St. Joseph River and lab strains of E. coli differ in their initial levels of antibiotic resistance?
St. Joe River Strain
Lab strain population
Hypothesis: Chemicals in river or antibiotics taken by the host may select for more resistant bacteria in the environment
Exposed to various stressors
Not exposed to stressors, but with genetic variations

Question 2: Can E. coli exposed to low (sub-inhibitory) concentrations of tetracycline evolve a greater resistance to tetracycline?
Natural selection
process
Tetracycline
Are they now more resistant to Tet?
Hypothesis: E. coli population can become more resistant to tetracycline if it is constantly exposed to it by the process of natural selection.

Question 3: When E. coli develops resistance to one antibiotic, does it also develop resistance to other antibiotics that it has not been directly exposed to? (cross-resistance)
Natural selection
process
Tetracycline
Can they develop cross resistance to other antibiotics?
Other antibiotics
Hypothesis: E. coli selected by one antibiotic may develop cross-resistance to other antibiotics due to a common resistance mechanism.

What is known about drug resistance in E. coli?
A gram negative bacterium (have two membranes)
Efflux pumps
Normal function is to keep out the toxic compounds in the environment, to send molecules out to interact with the host
In the presence of antibiotics, can be selected to become drug or multidrug efflux pumps
The surface channels can also decrease in numbers to keep the drug out
Li et al (2015) DOI: https://doi.org/10.1128/CMR.00117-14 [https://doi.org/10.1128/CMR.00117-14]

Prediction for multi-drug resistance in E. coli
Prediction: If tetracycline exposure caused an increase in efflux pump production, and/or a decrease in inward channels on the membrane, then this E. coli cell could also keep other drugs out and display cross-resistance (or multi-drug resistance)
Li et al (2015) DOI: https://doi.org/10.1128/CMR.00117-14 [https://doi.org/10.1128/CMR.00117-14]

Summary of Experimental Questions
Question 1: Do the St. Joseph River (SJ) and lab strains of E. coli differ in their initial levels of antibiotic resistance?
Test SJ strain and lab strain
Question 2: Can E. coli exposed to low (sub-inhibitory) concentrations of tetracycline evolve a greater resistance to tetracycline?
Question 3: Can the antibiotic-exposed E. coli develop cross resistance to other antibiotics even though E. coli was not directly exposed to them?

Day 4 (Wednesday) Transfer #4: transfer to control plate and Tet1
Day 5: (Thursday) Perform ZOI test with three antibiotic disks
Day 6 (Lab 12)
Take final measurements of ZOI; report data

Avoid digging into the agar - will trap your cultures and fail to form streaks

Use the side bench in NS154

group name, date, transfer number, (e.g. T2) and E. coli strain ( E. coli W), control (No tet ) or treatment (Tet0.37)

Streak the new plate in a radiant pattern
Do the same for the treatment plate
Incubate both T2 plates at 37 C overnight. Put away two old T1 plates in the fridge.

Day 2 (Part II)
Also need to measure zone of inhibition for each antibiotic disk (blank disk should be zero)

The size of zone of inhibition indicates the degree of resistance
Zone of inhibition:
Measure diameter of clear area ( including the disc ) ~24 hr after seeding (swabbing) the plate and adding the disc
Millimeters ( mm ) are commonly used as the unit
Large zone of inhibition means?
Small zone of inhibition means?
If circle is not complete, measure radius, then calculate D:   D = 2 x r
Measure from the clear edge

Measure zone of inhibition in millimeters (mm)
This is 35 mm, not 3.5 mm!!!
1. Use the metric side of the ruler.
2. Pay attention to the unit (millimeter, mm).
3. If your number is smaller than 6 mm, then you made a mistake in measuring the ZOIs

Experimental design
Independent variable (control treatment and experimental treatments)
Dependent variable?
Examples of controlled factors (confounding factors) in this experiment?
Replication?
Questions and predictions?

The role of natural selection in developing drug resistance
In the presence of the drug, drug-resistant bacteria can grow while the drug-sensitive ones cannot
Over time, due to the constant selective pressure (constant use of drugs), the bacterial population will become more resistant
drugs
drugs

Question: Can E. coli evolve resistance (by the process of natural selection) to tetracycline after repeated exposure to a low concentration of Tet?
Prediction: If bacteria become more resistant to the antibiotic  due to exposure to it (natural selection), then we should see a decrease in zone of inhibition around the antibiotic disk.

Hardy Weinberg Assumptions Predict no change in allele frequency if meet all assumptions
NO Natural selection
NO Mutation
NO Migration (i.e. no gene flow)
NO Genetic drift (i.e. large population size)
Random mating

Fitness
Fitness = relative measure of reproductive success of individuals within one species
4/4 = 1.0
1/4 = 0.25
